learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate a solid understanding of the context for the emergence of contemporary womens writing
Display a reasonable knowledge and understanding of some of the major writers, techniques, and theorists comprising womens writing and feminist literary theory
Practise advanced textual analysis of both literary and critical works
Demonstrate advanced research and writing skills
Demonstrate good oral and written communication skills
Undertake and complete an extended research project resulting in a substantial critical essay.

assessments.category: Essay/ Critique, Participation/ Student contribution, Reflection
assessments.task_description:
Rationale: This is to reward consistent work before and during the seminar, and to encourage the basic skills of literary scholarship (reading the set texts beforehand, bringing them to the seminar, expressing complex thoughts in written form, and working in the seminar). The task allows staff to gauge your engagement in the course and if you are showing difficulties with course material. In addition, better quality seminars result, and we have an assessment item that is identity invigilated.

For weeks 2, 4, 6, 7, 9, 10, 12, 13 you will be given 2 percentage points for each class you are prepared for, and participate in.

To gain the mark for each week students are required to do all of the following:

  1. arrive on time and stay for the entire seminar.
  2. bring copies (preferably print) of the novel and theoretical/critical reading for that week.
  3. complete a writing task before class that covers the week's required reading. The writing task will be made available via Blackboard on the Friday before it is due. Your response should be submitted through the provided link. Your response will be assessed on a pass/fail basis. You will need to write between 75-150 words.


If one or more of these elements are not satisfactory, no marks are awarded for that week's seminar. If you pass all 8 seminars, you will be awarded the full 16 marks.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Weekly writing exercises are to be submitted through the provided link in each week's Learning Resources folder on Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: Late submission is not possible for this piece of assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
Task: Choose one of the questions (to be provided on Blackboard) to write a clearly argued and researched academic essay of 2000 words in length (excluding quotations) that compares and contrasts two novels from the first two units (Weeks 1–7). 

Rationale: This task allows you to apply your research, writing, and analytical abilities to texts studied on the course thus far, so as to show your understanding of issues raised throughout the first two units, and of the primary texts themselves. It also provides a foundation for your final essay in terms of research essay skills and comparative textual analysis.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit via TurnItIn through Course Blackboard site.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
This in-person final examination will cover the third and fourth modules of the course.


Rationale:

This exam examines your understanding of the required readings and theoretical approaches in modules three and four, textual analysis abilities, and writing skills under exam conditions. It is also an identity-verified form of assessment.


Format:

  • 120 minutes, 10 minutes perusal.
  • The examination has two questions requiring you to write a short essay in response. Each essay is worth 20 marks (or 20 per cent of your marks for the course)
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.
